abstract: The accumulation of high nitrate concentrations in global water resources poses a severe threat to both environmental stability and human health, necessitating the development of robust remediation technologies. Single-atom alloys (SAAs) have recently emerged as a transformative bimetallic architecture for various catalytic applications, including the nitrate reduction reaction (NRR). In this work, we identify a profound divergence between thermocatalytic (T-NRR) and electrocatalytic (E-NRR) pathways, leading to starkly different performance profiles for SAA catalysts. Leveraging a series of Pd/Cu nanoalloys with Pd:Cu ratios ranging from 1:100 to 100:1, we find that the Pd/Cu<sub>(1:100)</sub> SAA achieves exceptional performance in E-NRR, characterized by superior activity (TOF<sub>Pd</sub> = 2 min<sup>-1</sup>) and high N<sub>2</sub> selectivity (94%). Conversely, this same SAA configuration performs poorly under thermocatalytic conditions compared to its more Pd-rich nanoalloy counterparts. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations reveal that the enhanced E-NRR performance of Pd/Cu<sub>(1:100)</sub> stems from the increased stability of NO<sub>3</sub>* intermediates and a lower kinetic barrier for N<sub>2</sub> formation relative to NH. These advantages are driven by localized pH shifts and the facilitated extraction of protons from water molecules at the electrochemical interface. This study elucidates the critical mechanistic distinctions between T-NRR and E-NRR, providing a framework for the rational design of SAA catalysts tailored for specific catalytic environments.
